item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can this affect animals btw? Is it only humans? …Well now I'm not just wondering if that's true, if prehistoric creatures are in there too, but maybe there's at least 1 massive corpse in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You do not recognize the bodies in the snow.
+1

item.title: Junji Ito vibes
item.content:encoded:
Nice combo of zombie tropes, hallucination, and general horror. A great, simple idea executed incredibly well!
+1000000000000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: deliciously unsettling, one of the best reads in a while +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not really creepy, and it's so unbelievable that it's not frightening, either. I'm not feeling anything at all, really, which is the death knell for an SCP. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rounderhouse unlike the corpses, you really have no chill, do you?

Just kidding, here have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: as someone with a bit of a fascination regarding this subject — the whole mountaineering obsession with summiting Everest despite the grave risks inherent in such an undertaking; the way they all point to how Mallory replied simply 'because it's there' when asked why he wanted to summit Everest; the numerous corpses they've been unable to move and how some of them have been imbued with a sort of fame (ex. green boots) simply for dying on the mountain; that mountaineers can end up just casually walking by on their way to the summit esp if the snow has melted enough; the way people who really have no business summiting Everest are being permitted to do so if they throw enough money at the endeavour; I could go on — so this was a treat for me. contrary to others, I liked the Edmund Hillary hat tip. :-) an easy +1 for me given that I already have an interest in this subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: never quite understood the appeal of this one. its just a bunch o dead people on a mountain. i assume the shtick is better understood in the mission record. otherwise nah didnt like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you calculate by volume instead of mass, you get 432 billion to 10 trillion depending on the different estimations of where Mount Everest actually starts. Does it start at sea level, at base camp, somewhere in between? How wide is it? If these numbers are actually a lot smaller, we could get a more reasonable amount of bodies, like 10 billion or so. And this is assuming the bodies are tightly packed, they could be including some snow or other rocks inside the body as body mass, despite not being part of the body. Or they aren't as tightly packed as possible. Given all these possibilities, the numbers could be more realistic than a trillion bodies.

item.title: No idea why this is so well rated
item.content:encoded:
The concept isn't particularly interesting, the addendum is pointless and half of it is just the header of the messages with something redacted for no apparent reason. Also it doesn't make sense that the snow hasn't melted until 2024 even though it's in contact with billions of corpses at 10°C

-1

item.title: Holy shit this is the one where I realized the punchline before it hits
item.content:encoded:
Like as soon as I read "The mountain is danger" at the end of the message log I was like: 'Oh hell no, is it going where I think it's going?' And it sure did. I actually felt that the final addendum kinda spoiled the fun for me as an extraneous explanation, but I suppose I am in the minority who picked up on the mystery from the get go.

Anyway, when it clicks so right with me mid-article, it means the set-up was absolutely perfect. Written in old school SCP style, best article of the year for me so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternatively, Mount Everest is every human to ever climb Mount Everest, retroactively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this article. That wham line at the end reminds me of classic SCPs from previous series. Also, color coding the logs in the first addendum helped with keeping track of the conversation. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my new favorite articles, the ending hits like a bus. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll never -1 a post as long as it's well written. Downvoting because it doesn't fit my taste has always seemed silly and petty. I no-voted. I can't 100% put my finger on it, but it didn't read "Foundation-y" (?) enough to me. Maybe it was the use of full names of MTF members? I don't know. I liked it. I really did. Just seemed off in tone for a Foundation document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 45 percent freezed my blood. upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woof. That last line hits like a truck. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well it looks like 1529 isn't lonely anymore, and that Hilary should have watched his… Steppe.
I really enjoyed this, where did the idea come from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Awesome!
Quiet, moderate, beautiful with a strong ending. I can totally imagine the wind on the Everest over the voice in my mind.
Not very complex, but surely well exhibited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: great concept, great execution, a bit short but the images and overall presentation make up for that imo, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending was the cherry on top of this skip.
+1

item.title: Re: Heat Transfer Rate?
item.content:encoded: Immediately, yes; that's how it kills people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first vote, yay!

Such a simple idea, well-written, and the conversation in the addendum adds a meaty dose horror to what is already a creepy phenomenon, especially as the Foundation explained it away as a hallucination. Very nice.

item.title: Heat Transfer Rate?
item.content:encoded:
Novote because...
Novote until I figure out the thermodynamics...

…they will absorb it without raising their temperature, which rests at approximately 10°C (50°F).

PATRICK TOUCHED ONE, ME N ARNOLD BURIED HIM

Does the latter sentence mean people in physical contact with SCP-5140 will be immediately cooled to 10°C? I thought the temperature would be gradually lowered when I was reading the first sentence and it confused me for a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this explanation. It also raises an interesting question. Is the foundation capable of recreating 5140 or are the instances able to survive the end of the world?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"everest? this better not be about the dang bodies or I swear to-GOD DAMMIT"

thanks and also fuck you this is horrifying I am scared of mountains +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's just what it is — a reference. A nice little nod to a bit of relevant history for the few keen-eyed readers who do notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the only possibility I can think is that due to the amount of times SCP 2000 may have been used to prevent end of the world scenarios the amount of people that have ever lived is much higher than in our world

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Roughly 714 billion corpses. My jaw dropped upon finishing the article and then again after calculating this. Makes you wonder what eldrich horror is responsible for it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn that last sentence just hits the nail on head immediately. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A nice change of pace from the 5000 contest, easy upvote.

item.title: Re: Is SCP-1529 connected to SCP-5140?
item.content:encoded: Welp, too late. This is my headcanon now.

item.title: Re: Is SCP-1529 connected to SCP-5140?
item.content:encoded: Nope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should say that I usually don't comment on articles and I don't want to knock the story at all, I love these old school creepy exploration shorts and it was just wonderfully paced for that. I just wanted to know why did you make the choice to even make the reference, as I don't (personally) find it to have any net positives?

item.title: Is SCP-1529 connected to SCP-5140?
item.content:encoded: I've noticed that SCP-1529 and this one has the similar ability of lowering the body temperature of their victims and since they're are from the same place (Mt Everest) it couldn't be a coincidence. Maybe they are related?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, well then.
– hide block

45% of Mt. Everest's mass is formed by corpses

That'll be a +1.

item.title: very cool
item.content:encoded: I like the like the ending very much It was a nice twist that makes me very intrigued +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Cool. Very scary and morbid. The comms log is a nice change from the ordinary. And the end… Suspension of disbeleif is holding for now so no problem with the math. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really think you'd be in the minority here, over the people that read this and even registered what the names were a reference to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Seems I missed a thing or two.

item.title: Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
Header image is "File:Everest North Face toward Base Camp Tibet Luca Galuzzi 2006.jpg" (CC BY-SA 2.5, Luca Galuzzi) from Wikimedia Commons.

Addendum image is seemingly a modified version of "File:Panoramique mont Everest.jpg" (public domain, Fabien1309) from Wikimedia Commons, but I'm not so sure about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending is a little goofy for my taste, but whatever. The exploration log was well-done and intriguing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
X140 skip and no Daeva? 0/10.

But in all seriousness, I find the concept interesting if a little morbid. However, the formatting of the Smith’s entries bug me. I get some are meant to just be abbreviations and that it should devolve a little as he panics, but it still feels weird.

No vote for now. Will come back to it later and see if I feel different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff, laconically terrifying. But the surnames of the team members felt so ham-fisted and took me completely out of it. To each their own, sure, but I wonder if that obvious of a reference is really necessary and what does it give to the story. For me it just made me think of the extraction team being named Bill Norgays and Peter Tenzings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is some classic camp science-fiction horror, with all the ambiguity and fantastical impossibility that I like to see in such a piece. It's all the better for it's ridiculousness, and utilizes the Foundation as a great straight-man to accentuate it all. +1 for sure.

item.title: Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
Awful lot of meat here.

Upon initial read, the rather "spartan" nature of the ConProcs and Desc struck me well. They are simple to follow and they get the point across. And more importantly, I did not feel as if you were being cheeky to hide a twist later.

The first addendum does a lot of lifting for this piece. I don't hate the whole console thing as much as I feared I would upon first seeing it and you do not overstay your welcome. The exchanges are "believeable" and terse and going with not-green, color-coded terminals was a good move.

The second addendum is, perhaps, too short. But, after sitting on it awhile long and considering the depth of this addendum in the context of the "fridge"-like nature of the rest of this piece, I am good with what you've pulled off.

This is an easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like scps that feed into real life

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We all have to climb a mountain someday, though whether we make it back down is another story entirely.

+1, very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess everybody ends up on Mt. Everest one day.

Amazing way to tie real life threats to our own life currently to a really creepy SCP. Absolutely adored the building tension between the MTF and the Base Camp.

+1'd, incredibly tense and implicitly terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, sweet, and loaded with fridge logic. Good stuff, rounder. This was my kinda skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At 45% it's more like 108 times the global population.

Edit: just noticed you did "all humans who have ever lived". i am blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what to say here :x I guess I didn't find the base idea very interesting, and the ending did nothing for me because all I could think about is how ridiculous it was. -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Erie, intriguing, and after reading that last sentence I had no choice but to upvote.

A strong +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh okay then.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: once again, math is the downfall of us all

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending is utterly unbelievable — the average person weighs 60 kilograms and Everest is almost 160 trillion, you'd need like 16 times as many corpses on Everest than there are humans that have existed on Earth — but its Ito-esque nature compensates for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
arthur post

this was a long idea, but it ended up being a short article. thanks to a couple people for looking this over - elogee, aismallard, and jackal - who also made the image in the last collapsible, thanks jackal! - come to mind. this isn't a permanent fixture, i'm just keeping the slot warm for a friend.

images:

+ show block
– hide block

header: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Everest_North_Face_toward_Base_Camp_Tibet_Luca_Galuzzi_2006.jpg — cc by sa 2.5
last image: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest#/media/File:Everest_kalapatthar.jpg — cc by sa 2.5 — edited by JackalRelated

arthur page

item.title: Beat to an Authorpost
item.content:encoded: Good article (I also made the FLIR Image!). Read a while back, just as good as I remember. +1
